def solve():
    input_data = sys.stdin.read().split()
    idx = 0
    t = int(input_data[idx]); idx += 1
    for _ in range(t):
        n = int(input_data[idx]); idx += 1
        a = list(map(int, input_data[idx:idx+n])); idx += n
        
        # Check: a[i] and (i+1) must have same parity for all i
        possible = True
        for i in range(n):
            if (a[i] % 2) != ((i + 1) % 2):
                possible = False
                break
        if not possible:
            print(-1)
            continue
        
        ops = []
        
        def rev(p):
            a[:p] = a[:p][::-1]
            ops.append(p)
        
        def bring_to_front(pos, limit):
            # bring a[pos] to index 0, using only odd-length reversals <= limit
            if pos == 0:
                return
            if (pos + 1) % 2 == 1:  # pos+1 is odd
                rev(pos + 1)
            else:
                # pos+1 is even, pos is even (0-indexed), so pos is even
                # reverse pos (odd since pos is even... pos even means pos is even, not odd)
                # pos is even means pos+1 is odd - contradiction. 
                # pos+1 even means pos is odd (0-indexed)
                # Try: reverse 1 first (no-op), then pos is still odd
                # Instead: reverse (pos) which is odd
                rev(pos)  # pos is odd length, brings a[pos-1] to front, a[pos] moves to index 1
                rev(1)    # no-op
                # a[pos] is now at index 1, reverse length 3? No...
                # Actually after rev(pos), original a[pos] is at index 1
                rev(3) if 3 <= limit else rev(1)
                # This doesn't work cleanly. Use a different approach:
        
        end = n
        while end >= 3:
            big = end
            small = end - 1
            
            # Place big at index end-1
            pos = a.index(big)
            if pos != end - 1:
                if (pos + 1) % 2 == 0:  # pos is odd (0-indexed), pos+1 even
                    rev(pos)  # odd length, now big is at index 1
                    pos = a.index(big)
                rev(pos + 1)  # now odd, big at front
                rev(end)      # end is odd, big goes to index end-1
            
            # Place small at index end-2
            pos = a.index(small)
            if pos != end - 2:
                if pos == end - 1:
                    rev(end - 2)  # end-2 is odd (end odd => end-2 odd)
                    pos = a.index(small)

                    pos = a.index(small)

                # Then reverse end-2 again...

import sys
input = sys.stdin.readline

def solve():
    n = int(input())
    a = list(map(int, input().split()))
    
    # Check solvability: a[i] (1-indexed value) must have same parity as position (1-indexed)
    # i.e., a[i] % 2 == (i+1) % 2 for all i in 0..n-1
    possible = True
    for i in range(n):
        if (a[i] % 2) != ((i + 1) % 2):
            possible = False
            break
    
    if not possible:
        print(-1)
        return
    
    ops = []
    
    def reverse_prefix(length):
        a[:length] = a[:length][::-1]
        ops.append(length)
    
    def bring_to_front(idx, end):
        # Bring a[idx] to front within first `end` elements
        if idx == 0:
            return
        if idx % 2 == 0:
            # idx+1 is odd
            reverse_prefix(idx + 1)
        else:
            # idx is odd, idx+2 is odd
            # After reverse(idx+2): element at idx goes to position 1
            reverse_prefix(idx + 2)
            # Now element is at position 1, reverse prefix of length 3
            reverse_prefix(3)
    
    end = n  # 1-indexed length of unsorted portion
    
    while end >= 3:
        # Place value `end` at position `end` (1-indexed)
        val_big = end
        idx_big = a.index(val_big)
        bring_to_front(idx_big, end)
        # Now val_big is at front, reverse prefix of length `end`
        reverse_prefix(end)
        # val_big is now at position end-1 (0-indexed), i.e., position end (1-indexed)
        
        # Place value `end-1` at position `end-1` (1-indexed)
        val_small = end - 1
        idx_small = a.index(val_small)
        # idx_small must be within first end-1 elements
        bring_to_front(idx_small, end - 1)
        # Now val_small is at front, reverse prefix of length `end-1`
        reverse_prefix(end - 1)
        # val_small is now at position end-2 (0-indexed), i.e., position end-1 (1-indexed)
        
        end -= 2
    
    # Check if sorted
    if a != list(range(1, n + 1)):
        print(-1)
        return
    
    print(len(ops))
    if ops:
        print(*ops)
    else:
        print()

t = int(input())
for _ in range(t):
    solve()
